nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, clear-to-slightly-turbid streams and river margins with sandy or fine-gravel substrate; shaded by riparian vegetation; often in slow-moving or still marginal pools adjacent to main current

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (essential — protects sensitive barbels)
leaf litter for cover and tannins
dense planting at margins (Java fern, Vallisneria, Echinodorus)
shaded areas with minimal direct light
caves or overhangs for daytime refuge

diet.notes: Bottom-feeding omnivore. Accepts sinking pellets and wafers, frozen/freeze-dried foods (bloodworm, daphnia, brine shrimp, tubifex), and micro-pellets. Forages continuously through substrate for organic detritus and small invertebrates. Should not be relied upon as a sole 'tank cleaner' — targeted feeding is required. Aquarium Co-Op recommends sinking pellets reaching the bottom before other fish consume them.

avoidWith: rough substrate (damages barbels, increases disease risk)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Corydoras trilineatus; shows typical reticulated head pattern and lateral line stripes diagnostic for the species vs. true C. julii.
Wikimedia Commons photograph of C. trilineatus in aquarium setting; good lateral view.

summary: The False Julii Cory (Corydoras trilineatus) is one of the most popular and widely traded small corydoras. Nearly all fish sold as 'Corydoras julii' in the aquarium trade are actually this species. A peaceful, hardy, bottom-dwelling shoaler ideal for community aquaria with a sandy substrate.
careNotes: TRADE IDENTITY NOTE — IMPORTANT: Virtually all fish sold in the aquarium trade as 'Corydoras julii' or 'Julii Cory' are actually Corydoras trilineatus. True Corydoras julii (Steindachner, 1906) is a rare export from the lower Amazon (Pará state, Brazil) and is seldom if ever seen in retail stores. The two species are distinguishable: C. trilineatus has a reticulated (net-like) pattern on the head and well-defined, connected horizontal stripes along the flank that merge into a reticulated or chain-like pattern; true C. julii has discrete, isolated spots on the head and flanks that do NOT form connected lines or a reticulated network. Aquarists should assume fish labeled 'C. julii' are C. trilineatus unless provenance can be confirmed. TAXONOMIC NOTE: The 2024 Dias & Britto revision proposed transferring a large number of Corydoras species (including C. trilineatus) to the resurrected genus Hoplisoma. As of early 2026 this reclassification remains contested; SeriouslyFish and FishBase continue to list the species under Corydoras trilineatus pending broader community acceptance. This entry uses the currently accepted name C. trilineatus. CARE: Corydoras trilineatus is a robust, beginner-friendly species best kept in groups of 6 or more — shoaling behavior is strongly expressed and isolated individuals become stressed and inactive. A fine sand substrate is non-negotiable: coarse gravel abrades the delicate barbels and skin, leading to bacterial infections that are difficult to treat. Keep barbels intact as a health indicator — eroding barbels signal substrate or water quality problems. Water should be soft to moderately hard and slightly acidic to neutral; avoid strongly alkaline conditions. Corydoras breathe atmospheric air by surfacing — this is normal; ensure the surface is accessible and well-oxygenated. Prefers subdued lighting; floating plants or a dimmer tank promotes confident foraging. The species is copper-sensitive: avoid copper-based medications (many ich treatments); treat with fish-safe alternatives. Water temperature should not exceed 26–27 °C for extended periods. Regular partial water changes (25–30% weekly) maintain the clean water this species demands.
breedingNotes: Corydoras trilineatus has been bred in captivity and is considered moderately achievable by an experienced hobbyist. Conditioning with high-protein live or frozen foods (bloodworm, tubifex) stimulates spawning. A cool water change of 2–4 °C (simulating rain-season conditions) often triggers spawning behavior. The female deposits adhesive eggs in small batches — typically 10–15 at a time — on plant leaves, glass, or hardscape; the male fertilizes them in a characteristic T-position embrace. Total egg output per spawning event is 100–300 eggs. Eggs hatch in 3–5 days at 24–25 °C; parents should be removed to prevent egg predation. Fry are free-swimming within a week and accept micro-worms and powdered fry food. Raise fry over fine sand and perform frequent small water changes to prevent ammonia spikes in the fry tank.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras trilineatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (4.0–5.0 cm SL), temperature (22–26 °C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (18–179 ppm / approx. 1–10 °dH), native range (Peru, upper Amazon tributaries), habitat, diet, breeding behavior, and detailed taxonomic notes distinguishing C. trilineatus from true C. julii. Also notes pending Hoplisoma reclassification (Dias & Britto 2024).

additional.name: FishBase — Corydoras trilineatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 6.0 cm TL), temperature range (22–26 °C), native range (Peru, Colombia, Brazil — upper Amazon basin), taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes), and lifespan data. FishBase records max size 6.0 cm TL vs SeriouslyFish 5.0 cm SL; used FishBase TL maximum as adultSizeCm.max. Both sources confirm species distribution includes Peru (Ucayali, Marañón systems) and parts of the upper Amazon basin.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Corydoras Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: Used for trade care recommendations: sinking foods required, group size minimum 6, sand substrate importance for barbel health, copper sensitivity warning, and community compatibility notes. Aquarium Co-Op does not have a species-specific page for C. trilineatus but their general corydoras guide is widely cited in the hobby and addresses the julii/trilineatus mislabeling issue.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Corydoras trilineatus species profile
additional.notes: Used for breeding notes (T-position spawning, egg count 100–300, hatching at 3–5 days), minimum group size recommendation (6+), and trade mislabeling commentary. Confirmed temperature ceiling of 26 °C for long-term health. Also documents that the Hoplisoma reclassification (Dias & Britto 2024) proposes transferring this species but it is not yet universally adopted.

additional.name: Dias, A.C. & Britto, M.R. (2024) — Hoplisoma revision (referenced via SeriouslyFish/PFK)
additional.notes: The 2024 Dias & Britto revision proposed moving many Corydoras spp. (including trilineatus) to Hoplisoma based on phylogenetic analysis. As of June 2026 this transfer is noted but not universally adopted; FishBase and SeriouslyFish still list the species as Corydoras trilineatus. Citation is via the SeriouslyFish page which summarizes the revision. Direct paper URL not publicly available via open access at time of research.

fish.breedingNotes: Bred in captivity with moderate effort. Condition on live/frozen foods; trigger with a 2–4 °C cool water change. Female deposits adhesive eggs in batches of 10–15 in T-position; 100–300 eggs per spawn. Eggs hatch in 3–5 days; fry free-swimming within a week and accept micro-worms and powdered food. Remove parents to prevent egg predation.
